name: Bern
history: Bern was founded in 1191 by Berchtold V, Duke of Zähringen, and quickly became an important trading center in the Swiss plateau. The city joined the Swiss Confederation in 1353 and gradually expanded its territory through military campaigns and strategic alliances during the medieval period. In 1848, Bern was chosen as the capital of the newly formed Swiss Federal State, a role it has maintained ever since. The city's Old Town, with its characteristic arcaded streets and sandstone buildings, was largely constructed between the 15th and 18th centuries and remains remarkably well-preserved today.
culturalNotes: Bern embodies a distinctly Swiss blend of precision, understated elegance, and civic pride, reflected in its meticulously maintained medieval architecture and public spaces. The city is known for its relaxed yet cultured atmosphere, with a strong tradition of direct democracy, local markets, and respect for heritage that permeates daily life. The famous Zytglogge clock tower and the wandering bears in the Bärengraben represent enduring symbols of Bernese identity and continuity.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit in spring or early autumn to enjoy comfortable temperatures and see the city's medieval architecture without peak summer crowds.
languageNotes: The local language is Swiss German, specifically the Bernese dialect (Bärndütsch), which differs noticeably from standard German spoken in neighboring countries. While most residents speak German, French, and English, learning basic phrases like "Grüezi" (hello) and "Merci" (thank you) is appreciated by locals and helps visitors connect with the community.

places.name: Bern Old Town
places.description: A stunning medieval UNESCO World Heritage site featuring the iconic Zytglogge clock tower, arcaded streets, and well-preserved 15th-century architecture. This is the atmospheric heart of Bern where you'll find cobblestone streets, local shops, restaurants, and the best sense of the city's character and history.

activities.detail: Watch the famous astronomical clock perform its hourly show with mechanical figures, then climb the tower for panoramic views of the Old Town and surrounding cityscape.
Visit the modest apartment where Albert Einstein lived from 1902-1909 while developing his theory of relativity, now a small museum displaying memorabilia and documents.
Stroll beneath the covered porticoes (some dating to the 15th century) that line the main thoroughfares, offering shelter while shopping and discovering hidden courtyards.
Admire this Gothic masterpiece with its ornate main portal and climb the 344 steps of the tower for sweeping views of the Aare River and Alps.
Tour Switzerland's seat of government featuring impressive Renaissance architecture and learn about Swiss political history from knowledgeable local guides.
Browse Tuesday and Saturday farmers' markets for regional produce, cheese, and crafts, then explore independent boutiques tucked into medieval alleyways.
Visit the mascot bears of Bern in their natural riverside habitat, a tradition dating back to the 16th century celebrating the city's namesake.

food.detail: A hearty traditional dish featuring cured meats, sausages, and potatoes, historically served to farmers and now a staple in local restaurants throughout the Old Town.
Crispy potato pancakes, often topped with cheese or bacon, representing Swiss-German comfort food culture and widely available at casual eateries.
Experience melted cheese served in a communal pot at traditional fondue restaurants, using bread to dip and socializing as locals do during winter evenings.
Spiced gingerbread cookies unique to Bern, sold in specialty shops and markets, often decorated with elaborate icing and traditional motifs.
Grilled Swiss sausages available from street vendors and casual restaurants, paired with mustard and fresh bread as a quick authentic lunch.

food.text: Meringue with Double Cream (Meringue aux Deux Crèmes)
food.detail: A beloved local dessert of crispy meringue topped with generous portions of whipped cream and fruit, found in cafés and pastry shops throughout the district.

culture.detail: Swiss culture values precision and respect for quiet times; keep noise minimal in the early morning and late evening, and expect shops to close promptly at posted times.
Bernese German (Bernertütsch) is the local dialect, though most residents speak standard German and English; greet shopkeepers and service staff with 'Grüezi' for a warm reception.
While cards are accepted, smaller shops and markets prefer Swiss francs and tips of 5-10% are appreciated but not obligatory—rounding up is a common practice.
The Old Town is a living neighborhood, not just a museum; avoid eating while walking, use designated crossing areas, and be respectful when photographing locals and their homes.

places.name: Bern Cathedral
places.description: Switzerland's tallest Gothic church at 101 meters, featuring stunning stained glass windows and intricate exterior sculptures. Visitors can climb the 254 steps for panoramic views over the old town and surrounding Aare River valley.

activities.detail: Ascend the 254 spiral steps to the top of the 101-meter spire for panoramic views over Bern's old town and the Aare River valley.
View the cathedral's intricate stained glass windows, including modern works by artists like Jürg Kreienbühl that blend with historical designs.
Examine the elaborate exterior stone carvings on the main portal, featuring the Last Judgment scene and detailed Gothic ornamentation that exemplifies 15th-century craftsmanship.
Experience performances on the cathedral's impressive Müller organ, which regularly hosts classical music concerts that resonate through the Gothic interior.
Visit the small museum within the cathedral precinct displaying religious artifacts, vestments, and historical documents related to the building's 500-year history.

activities.text: Walk the Old Town Surrounding Streets
activities.detail: Stroll through the UNESCO-listed medieval old town's narrow arcaded streets around Marktgasse and Kramgasse, which offer historic shops and cafés with views of the cathedral.

food.detail: A crispy potato pancake often topped with bacon, cheese, or fried egg, representing the hearty local cuisine of the Bernese region.
Egg noodles mixed with potatoes, onions, and alpine cheese from the surrounding mountain valleys, topped with fried onions and served in many Bern restaurants.
An open-faced bread and cheese sandwich baked with white wine and onions until golden, a warming specialty found in traditional Bernese establishments.
An assortment of cured meats, sausages, and cheeses sourced from local producers, typically served in restaurants near the cathedral district.
A spiced honey cookie from Bern's medieval tradition, sold in specialty shops throughout the old town and perfect as a souvenir with Alpine spices.

culture.detail: The cathedral remains an active place of worship, so visitors should be quiet and respectful during regular service times; check ahead for mass schedules before planning your visit.
As a sacred religious site, visitors are expected to wear modest clothing with covered shoulders and knees when entering the cathedral interior.

The cathedral's architecture and sculptures tell biblical stories in medieval German-speaking tradition; obtaining an audio guide or hiring a local guide enriches understanding of its religious and artistic significance.

culture.text: Visit During Light Hours for Tower Climb
culture.detail: The bell tower is only open during daytime hours and offers the best views in clear weather; plan your 254-step climb for late morning or early afternoon.

places.name: Federal Palace
places.description: The iconic seat of the Swiss government with its distinctive Renaissance dome visible across Bern's skyline. Free guided tours are available when parliament is not in session, offering insight into Swiss democracy and the building's impressive architecture.

activities.detail: Explore the National Council and Council of States chambers to witness where Swiss legislative debates take place, available when parliament is not in session.
View the building's iconic copper dome from the Bundesplatz forecourt, one of Bern's most recognizable architectural features visible across the skyline.
Walk through the expansive plaza in front of the palace featuring fountains, government buildings, and seasonal markets.
See the historic weapons and military regalia displayed in this ornate chamber within the palace.
Gain insight into Switzerland's unique political system and federalism through the palace's exhibitions and architectural design.

food.detail: A crispy potato pancake native to Bern, often topped with cheese or bacon, available at nearby restaurants around Bundesplatz.
A hearty traditional platter featuring boiled meats, sausages, and sauerkraut that represents classic Bernese cuisine.
Sample the famous Swiss cheese with large holes produced in the Emmental region near Bern at local markets and shops.
A spiced gingerbread cookie traditional to Bern, perfect for picking up at street vendors near the palace.
Enjoy Swiss coffee and pastries at outdoor cafés surrounding the square while overlooking the palace.

culture.detail: Verify when parliament is not in session before visiting, as free guided tours are only available during recess periods.
Photography may be restricted in certain chambers and areas during tours, so follow guide instructions carefully.
Wear modest clothing as you are entering Switzerland's most important political building and symbol of government.
The palace's architecture reflects Swiss political structure with the dome representing unity and the wings representing cantonal diversity.

places.name: Rose Garden
places.description: A peaceful hilltop garden with over 200 varieties of roses offering spectacular views over Bern's old town and the Aare River loop. It's an ideal spot for relaxation, photography, and understanding the city's geography from above.

activities.detail: Stroll through the garden's 200+ rose varieties arranged by type and color, with labeled specimens that bloom peak season May through October.
Capture sweeping views of Bern's medieval old town, the iconic cathedral spire, and the Aare River's distinctive loop from the hilltop vantage point.
Use the garden's elevated position to trace the Aare River's horseshoe bend below and spot the Nydeggbrücke bridge connecting the old town.
Settle on one of the garden's wooden benches among the rose beds for quiet reflection with views across the Swiss plateau.
Attend summer rose festivals and evening garden concerts held in the garden amphitheater area during peak bloom months.
Read the informational plaques throughout the garden to learn about rose cultivation, historical varieties, and their origins across Europe and Asia.

food.detail: A specialty local honey infused with rose petals from the Rosengarten, available at nearby gift shops and reflecting the garden's famous blooms.
A traditional herbal tea made from alpine rose petals and leaves, often enjoyed at outdoor cafés near the garden for a floral, refreshing experience.
Sample the region's famous semi-hard cheese at nearby Kirchenfeld restaurants, a pairing tradition for visitors enjoying views from Bern's elevated gardens.

culture.detail: Visit May through July for peak rose flowering; avoid winter months when the garden is less vibrant, though evergreen shrubs and structure remain.
Remain on designated paths, do not pick flowers or leaves, and keep noise levels respectful of the peaceful garden atmosphere cherished by locals.
While there is no formal entry fee, Swiss custom suggests rounding up small purchases at any café kiosk; tipping 5-10% is appreciated but not obligatory.

places.name: Einstein House
places.description: The modest apartment where Albert Einstein lived while developing his theory of relativity in 1905. This intimate museum offers a glimpse into Einstein's daily life and genius through exhibits explaining his groundbreaking work.

activities.detail: Walk through the carefully preserved two-room flat where Einstein lived from 1902 to 1909 and see period furniture and personal artifacts.
Study interactive displays explaining the theory of relativity and Einstein's 1905 'miracle year' discoveries through diagrams and multimedia presentations.
View short films about Einstein's life and scientific breakthroughs in the museum's screening room.
Browse books, documentaries, and souvenirs related to Einstein and modern physics in the ground floor shop.
Stroll the charming medieval Old Town streets surrounding Einstein House, lined with historic buildings, galleries, and cafés.
Visit the iconic Zytglogge clock tower a short walk away, which Einstein studied and used as a reference for his relativity experiments.

food.detail: A hearty traditional platter featuring smoked meats, sausages, and potatoes that represents classic Swiss-German cuisine from the region.
Crispy potato pancakes topped with melted Emmental or Gruyère cheese, a beloved Bernese comfort food.
Sample handmade chocolates from nearby confectioneries using traditional Swiss recipes and local Bernese cacao preparations.
Fresh grilled Swiss-style sausage served on crusty bread at street vendors throughout the Old Town.
A classic melted cheese dish from the nearby Neuchâtel region, typically served with bread and local white wine in Bern restaurants.

culture.detail: The museum is housed in a residential building, so maintain a quiet demeanor and follow posted guidelines about noise levels during visits.
Most locals in Bern speak German; greeting staff with 'Grüezi' (hello) or 'Danke' (thank you) is appreciated and shows respect for Swiss culture.
Einstein House closes early some days and all day Monday, so check hours before visiting to avoid disappointment.
While cards are widely accepted, carrying Swiss francs (CHF) is helpful for small purchases at the museum shop and nearby Old Town vendors.

places.name: Bernese Historical Museum
places.description: A comprehensive museum covering Bern's history from medieval times to the present, with impressive collections of tapestries, sculptures, and regional artifacts. The building itself is architecturally significant and offers context for understanding the city's cultural importance.

activities.detail: Explore one of Europe's finest collections of 15th and 16th-century tapestries, many depicting biblical and courtly scenes that showcase the wealth and artistic taste of Bern's patrician families.
View important stone and wooden sculptures spanning medieval to Renaissance periods, including works that once adorned Bern's cathedral and civic buildings.
Discover treasures from the 1476 Battle of Murten, including armor, weapons, and precious objects that Bern captured and have been preserved for over 500 years.
Examine traditional Bernese Oberland costumes and intricate embroidered textiles that illustrate local craftsmanship and cultural identity throughout the centuries.
Study the neo-Gothic facade and interior design of the 1892 museum building itself, which represents Bern's cultural aspirations during the late 19th century.

food.detail: This crispy potato pancake is Bern's most iconic comfort food, traditionally served with melted cheese, bacon, or a fried egg on top.
A hearty meat platter featuring sauerkraut, potatoes, and various smoked meats including beef, pork, and sausage, emblematic of Bernese cuisine.
A dense, spiced gingerbread cookie flavored with honey and nuts that has been a Bern specialty since medieval times, available at local bakeries.
The Swiss melted cheese dish is particularly popular in Bern restaurants, traditionally made with Emmental and Gruyère cheeses from surrounding regions.

culture.detail: Swiss museums expect hushed tones and minimal disturbance; speaking softly and avoiding phone use maintains the contemplative atmosphere locals appreciate.
Arrive on time for any guided tours or reserved entry slots, as Swiss culture highly values punctuality and organized scheduling.
Plan accordingly as the Bernisches Historisches Museum is closed on Mondays, following standard Swiss museum schedules.
English is spoken by staff, but many labels and descriptions are in German; having a translation app ready enhances understanding of regional artifacts and historical context.

places.name: Jungfrau
places.description: One of the most iconic peaks in the Alps, accessible via the mountain railway from Interlaken to the Jungfraujoch station at 3454 meters. The journey itself is spectacular, and the high-altitude station offers views of Europe's longest glacier and unforgettable panoramas.

activities.detail: Visit the 'Top of Europe' at 3454 meters to walk on the Aletsch Glacier, explore ice palaces, and enjoy panoramic views across the Alps.
Trek the scenic path between Eigergletscher and Alpiglen with dramatic views of the north face of the Eiger mountain.
Tour Europe's highest observatory at Jungfraujoch to learn about Alpine geology and astronomy with 360-degree mountain views.
Experience the UNESCO-listed cogwheel railway that climbs through tunnels and open sections with ever-changing Alpine landscapes.
Wander the charming Alpine village at the base with traditional Swiss chalets, mountain shops, and access to multiple hiking trails.
Hike across Europe's longest glacier with guides to safely explore crevasses and ice formations while learning about glacial dynamics.

food.detail: A melted cheese dish served in a communal pot, made with Emmental and Gruyère cheese, quintessential Swiss Alpine comfort food.
Crispy shredded potato pancakes often served with eggs, cheese, or bacon, a traditional Bernese staple found throughout mountain restaurants.
A hearty Alpine pasta dish mixed with potatoes, cheese, and onions, representing comfort food of mountain herders.
Melted cheese scraped onto a plate and served with bread and pickled vegetables, a warming dish perfect after mountain activities.
The famous Swiss cheese with distinctive holes, produced in the nearby Emmental Valley and available fresh at local markets.

culture.detail: The Jungfrau railway fills quickly during peak season (June-September), so purchase tickets in advance online or arrive early in the day.
At 3454 meters, temperatures drop significantly and UV exposure increases; locals and guides emphasize proper clothing regardless of valley weather.
Alpine conditions change rapidly, and visibility on Jungfraujoch can close access within minutes; follow posted alerts and don't venture beyond marked routes.
Greet hikers with 'Grüezi' on trails, stay on marked paths to protect fragile Alpine ecosystems, and respect private property around mountain villages.

places.name: Thun Castle
places.description: A 12th-century hilltop castle with striking white towers overlooking Lake Thun and the Bernese Oberland beyond. The castle offers museum exhibits on regional history and Swiss warfare, plus panoramic views from the tower and castle gardens.

activities.detail: Ascend the four distinctive white towers for 360-degree views of Lake Thun, the Bernese Oberland peaks, and the town below.
Browse exhibits on regional medieval history, Swiss warfare traditions, and artifacts spanning from the 12th century to modern times.
Wander the manicured grounds surrounding the castle with benches and viewpoints overlooking Lake Thun and the Alpine landscape.
Capture panoramic shots from the castle terrace, especially during golden hour when the white towers glow against the lake.
Browse local crafts, regional books, and Swiss souvenirs in the castle's on-site shop for authentic mementos.
Descend from the castle into Thun's Old Town and reach the lakeside promenade within 10 minutes for waterfront cafés and boat tours.

food.detail: Taste the famous Swiss hard cheese with distinctive holes, produced in the nearby Emmental region and served in local restaurants.
Sample this crispy potato pancake, a Bernese staple often topped with cheese, bacon, or a fried egg at castle cafés and nearby inns.
Enjoy fresh-caught perch from Lake Thun, typically pan-fried and served with lemon at waterfront restaurants below the castle.
Order this hearty regional combination of cured meats, sausages, and potatoes, perfect for lunch after exploring the castle.

food.text: Meringue with Cream (Meringues à la Crème)
food.detail: Indulge in the famous Bernese meringue dessert topped with whipped cream, sold in local pastry shops near the castle entrance.

culture.detail: The castle operates seasonal hours (typically April to October) and may close Mondays; check ahead as museum exhibits sometimes have rotating schedules.
The castle involves climbing steep stone tower staircases and cobbled pathways, so sturdy walking shoes are essential for safety and comfort.
Many visitors benefit from reading the provided English information boards or renting an audio guide to fully appreciate the 12th-century architecture and regional significance.
Combine your castle visit with a lake cruise or lakeside walk, as Thun is a hub for Bernese Oberland excursions departing from the harbor below.

places.name: Lake Thun
places.description: A pristine alpine lake surrounded by mountains, offering scenic boat cruises, lakeside villages, and water activities. The lake is dotted with charming towns and provides some of the most photogenic views in the Bern region.

activities.detail: Take a classic paddle-steamer or modern boat tour across Lake Thun to view the Bernese Oberland mountains and stop at lakeside villages.
Explore this 12th-century hilltop castle in Thun town with museum exhibits, tower views, and sweeping lake vistas.
Access paragliding, mountain biking, and hiking expeditions from this major resort town at the lake's eastern end.
Hike the scenic cliffside trail between Beatenberg and Merligen with dramatic lake and mountain panoramas.
Tour this waterfront medieval castle and enjoy swimming or sunbathing on the nearby public beach in the charming village of Spiez.
Take the funicular from Interlaken to this viewpoint, then hike trails with unobstructed views of Lake Thun and the Jungfrau region.
Visit this picturesque lakeside village famous for its church steeple reflected in the water and postcard-perfect mountain backdrop.
Enjoy windsurfing, kayaking, stand-up paddleboarding, and swimming at several designated beach areas around the lake.

food.detail: Fresh lake fish caught from Thun and prepared simply with butter and lemon, a regional delicacy found in lakeside restaurants.
Crispy potato pancakes topped with melted Emmental or Gruyère cheese, a hearty Bernese staple served throughout the region.
Cubed beef cooked in hot oil at the table, reflecting the Swiss alpine tradition popular in Thun's lakeside dining establishments.
Locally-caught trout often grilled whole or filleted and served with seasonal vegetables in waterfront establishments.
A traditional platter of cured meats, sausages, and smoked ham served with beans and potatoes, representing Bernese culinary heritage.
Sample handmade Swiss chocolate and pralines from artisan shops in Thun and nearby villages, showcasing regional confectionery craftsmanship.

culture.detail: The lake's medieval castles and towns tell stories of medieval trade routes and Swiss independence; local guides enthusiastically share this heritage.
Swiss culture values tranquility; avoid loud activities or noise during midday rest periods (typically noon to 2 PM) and evenings after 10 PM.
Tipping is not obligatory as service charges are included, but rounding up the bill or leaving 5-10% is appreciated for good service.
The region celebrates Swiss traditions through summer folk festivals and yodeling events; attending these offers authentic cultural immersion.

places.name: Grindelwald
places.description: A picturesque mountain village famous as a hiking base with the dramatic north face of the Eiger looming overhead. The village offers excellent trails, traditional Swiss chalets, and access to mountain railways and glaciers.

activities.detail: A spectacular moderate hiking path that descends from Eigergletscher station along the base of the Eiger's north face, offering unobstructed views of this iconic 3,970m peak.
Take the cogwheel railway from Grindelwald up to 3,454m where you can walk on the Aletsch Glacier, visit the Sphinx Observatory, and see panoramic Alpine vistas.
Accessed by gondola from Grindelwald, this high-altitude zone offers cliff walks, zip-lining, and hiking trails with sweeping valley views.
A short walk through a dramatic limestone gorge carved by glacial meltwater, featuring steep canyon walls and a turquoise stream.
A popular family-friendly trail leading to an alpine lake with mirror reflections of the Wetterhorn and Schreckhorn peaks.
One of Switzerland's most accessible glaciers, viewable via a gentle hiking trail that passes through forests and meadows before reaching the glacier's snout.

food.detail: A hearty Bernese Alpine pasta baked with potatoes, onions, and local cheese, traditionally served with apple compote.
A classic Swiss cheese fondue combining equal parts Gruyère and Emmental, perfect for après-hike warmth in Grindelwald's mountain restaurants.
Crispy potato pancakes topped with melted Valais raclette cheese, a favorite comfort dish in local mountain lodges.
Local mountain streams supply restaurants with freshly caught trout, often prepared simply with butter and Alpine herbs.
While originating from Zurich, this Swiss breakfast staple of rolled oats, yogurt, and fresh berries is widely served in Grindelwald guesthouses.

culture.detail: Always stay on marked paths, greet fellow hikers with 'Grüezi,' and be prepared for rapidly changing Alpine weather even on clear mornings.
Locals speak Swiss German dialect; simple phrases like 'Grüezi' (hello), 'Merci' (thanks), and 'Auf Wiedersehen' (goodbye) are appreciated and useful.

culture.text: Book mountain restaurants and cable cars in advance
culture.detail: During peak seasons (July-August and winter), popular lifts and dining spots fill quickly, so reserve ahead to avoid disappointment.
The region maintains active alpine dairy farming; respect livestock on trails, don't disturb cows, and support local cheese and dairy producers.

places.name: Bern
places.description: Switzerland's capital city features a UNESCO World Heritage Old Town with medieval architecture, charming arcaded streets, and the famous Bear Park. Visitors are drawn to its political significance, cultural institutions, and perfectly preserved historic character.

activities.detail: Observe Bern's iconic bears in a spacious, naturalistic habitat along the Aare River, a symbol of the city since medieval times.
Watch the ornate astronomical clock perform its mechanical show on the hour in the heart of the Old Town.
Explore this stunning Gothic cathedral with its intricate portal carvings and climb the tower for panoramic city views.
Stroll along the picturesque riverside paths with views of the old covered bridges and medieval ramparts.
Visit the modest apartment where Albert Einstein lived while developing his theory of relativity.
Tour the impressive seat of Swiss government with its Renaissance dome and learn about Switzerland's political system.
Browse the six kilometers of covered medieval arcades featuring local shops, cafes, and galleries.

food.detail: A hearty dish of cured meats, sausages, and potatoes that represents traditional Bernese cuisine.
Crispy potato pancakes served with cheese or bacon, a Swiss specialty beloved throughout Bern.
The creamy, nutty Swiss cheese produced in the nearby Emmental valley, perfect for fondue or casual tasting.
A rich, caramelized onion soup traditionally served during the November onion festival.
Spiced gingerbread cookies decorated with icing, a Bern specialty found in markets and cafes year-round.

culture.detail: Arrive on time for reservations and meetings, as Swiss culture values precision and reliability.
Respect official quiet hours, typically between 22:00 and 07:00, and avoid loud activities during these times.
While cards are widely accepted, many small cafes and markets prefer cash, so carry some Swiss francs.
Most shops close on Sundays, so plan your purchases accordingly and visit museums or outdoor attractions instead.

restaurants.name: Della Casa
restaurants.description: Legendary Bernese restaurant serving traditional Swiss cuisine in a historic 16th-century building with wood-paneled rooms.

restaurants.name: Stall6
restaurants.description: Modern take on Swiss classics in a stylish setting with excellent wine selection and creative preparations.

restaurants.name: Café Beaulieu
restaurants.description: Upscale French restaurant with refined cuisine, impeccable service, and an extensive wine list in an elegant setting.

restaurants.name: Restaurant Schloss Hünigen
restaurants.description: Castle restaurant overlooking the Aare River offering seasonal menus with a blend of Swiss and French influences.
restaurants.mustOrder: Slow-Cooked Beef with Red Wine Sauce

restaurants.name: Rosengarten
restaurants.description: Family-friendly traditional Swiss tavern with hearty portions, local specialties, and views of the old city.

restaurants.name: Tim Raue
restaurants.description: Innovative Asian fusion cuisine from renowned chef Tim Raue in a minimalist modern setting with theatrical presentations.

restaurants.name: Arteplage
restaurants.description: Trendy restaurant focusing on local, seasonal ingredients with creative preparations and a vibrant atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Restaurant Kornhauskeller
restaurants.description: Historic basement restaurant in a grand baroque building offering Swiss comfort food and international dishes at reasonable prices.

restaurants.name: Meridiano
restaurants.description: Elegant Italian restaurant with authentic Northern Italian cuisine, fresh pasta, and an impressive selection of Italian wines.

restaurants.name: Schwellenmätteli
restaurants.description: Casual riverside spot popular for grilled food, salads, and local beers with outdoor seating and relaxed atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Restaurant Altes Tramdepot
restaurants.description: Converted tram depot brewery restaurant with hearty Swiss food, craft beers, and a lively beer hall atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Gourmanderie Molière
restaurants.description: Sophisticated French bistro with seasonal tasting menus and attentive service near Parliament in an intimate setting.

restaurants.name: Verve
restaurants.description: Modern restaurant with Mediterranean influences, locally-sourced ingredients, and creative plating in a minimalist space.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pan-Seared Sea Bass with Seasonal Vegetables

restaurants.name: Restaurant Adler
restaurants.description: Traditional guild house restaurant with authentic Bernese specialties, wood-beamed ceilings, and historic atmosphere since 1902.

restaurants.name: Lory's Boutique Hotel Restaurant
restaurants.description: Intimate restaurant in a boutique hotel setting with creative seasonal menus and personalized service in a refined ambiance.
